policy_statement: Develop a list of the most carbon-intense building materials/products eligible for incentives or preferential procurement treatment via the interagency GreenNY Council.
mechanism_description: The interagency GreenNY Council develops a list of carbon-intense building materials eligible for incentives or preferential procurement treatment, steering purchases toward lower-carbon options and reducing construction-related emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Procurement standards → material substitution/low-carbon supply → ↓ embodied (lifecycle) GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Public agencies revise purchasing decisions for construction materials by defining/labeling high-carbon materials as targets for incentives or preferential procurement of lower-carbon alternatives. (2) Process: Demand-pull drives substitution to lower-GHG products and/or induces manufacturers to reduce process/energy intensity (e.g., less clinker, more recycled inputs, cleaner energy), lowering emissions per unit material. (3) Outcome: Reduced embodied emissions from construction supply chains (CO₂ from cement calcination and heat, steel production energy/process emissions), leading to net GHG reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: Shifting public (and influenced private) purchasing toward lower-embodied-carbon building materials reduces lifecycle industrial/process and energy emissions associated with producing construction materials.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: procurement criteria change material choices → lower lifecycle/embodied emissions from producing construction materials → mitigation.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while it can improve resource efficiency (e.g., recycled content) and spur innovation, the single biggest climate-relevant effect of the mechanism is direct GHG reduction from lower-carbon material production, not reduced energy use at the point of consumption or adaptation outcomes.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a procurement list were purely informational without altering purchasing or specifications, impacts could be indirect; however the stated mechanism explicitly ties the list to incentives/preferential treatment, implying a demand shift and emissions reduction.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: any policy using “preferential procurement for lower-embodied-carbon materials” has its primary climate effect through reduced embodied GHG emissions in the construction materials supply chain, which is mitigation.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: whether reductions come mainly from clinker substitution, recycled content, cleaner energy, or avoided virgin production depends on policy-level requirements and market context and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “preferential procurement” is implemented mainly to reduce waste/virgin material use (resource efficiency framing) without explicit GHG/LCA criteria, categorization could blur; but with carbon-intensity targeting as described, mitigation is dominant.
key_indicators: “interagency GreenNY Council”; “develop a list”; “most carbon-intense building materials and products”; “eligible for incentives”; “preferential treatment in procurement”
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as an information/disclosure instrument (“develop a list”), but because the list is explicitly tied to procurement preference and incentive eligibility, procurement-requirement is the best fit; direct emissions impact depends on subsequent adoption in contracts/program rules.
